brain_source_detail: 220+ essays spanning 2001-2025, plus interviews, talks, and tweets
brain_tagline: a curated collection of his most influential thinking on startups, programming, writing, taste, and doing great work
brain_bio: Paul Graham is a programmer, essayist, and co-founder of Y Combinator — the most successful startup accelerator in history. His essays on paulgraham.com have shaped how a generation of founders and programmers think about startups, ambition, wealth creation, and the craft of building things. He is one of the most cited thinkers in Silicon Valley.
topic_examples: startups, programming, writing, wealth, taste, ambition, fundraising, hackers, ideas, growth, education, design
quick_start_prompt: I'm a developer with a side project getting traction. Should I quit my job and go full-time on it?

skill_examples.advise: Should I drop out of college to work on my startup?
skill_examples.debate: Is it better to build something a lot of people kind of want, or something a few people really want?
skill_examples.apply: Apply the 'do things that don't scale' framework to my marketplace startup.
skill_examples.teach: Explain what makes a good startup idea using Paul's frameworks.
skill_examples.deep_dive: Deep dive into Paul's thinking on wealth and economics.
skill_examples.connect: Connect Paul's ideas about taste to hiring decisions.
skill_examples.brainfight: Move fast vs. do things right — which should win early on?
skill_examples.mashup: Mashup: startups + writing + programming as craft.
skill_examples.evolve: How has Paul's thinking on AI and programming evolved?

workflow_examples.learning: New to startups? Teach → deep-dive → evolve to understand how PG's advice has matured.
workflow_examples.research: Fundraising soon? Deep-dive fundraising atoms → apply to your situation → debate your assumptions.

startups_founding.name: Startups & Founding
startups_founding.description: How to start companies, find ideas, pick cofounders, and survive the early days. The core of PG's intellectual contribution.

programming_hackers.name: Programming & Hackers
programming_hackers.description: Programming as craft, hacker culture, Lisp, the nature of good code, and what separates great programmers from average ones.

writing_thinking.name: Writing & Thinking
writing_thinking.description: Writing as a tool for thinking, the craft of essays, how to write clearly, and why writing well matters for everything.

wealth_economics.name: Wealth & Economics
wealth_economics.description: How wealth is created (not divided), why startups are the optimal vehicle, and the economics of inequality, taxation, and incentives.

education_learning.name: Education & Learning
education_learning.description: Why school fails ambitious people, autodidactic learning, what you can't learn in college, and how curiosity drives real education.

design_taste.name: Design & Taste
design_taste.description: What good taste is, how to develop it, why design matters, and the relationship between beauty, simplicity, and quality.

silicon_valley.name: Silicon Valley & Culture
silicon_valley.description: Why startup hubs form, what makes Silicon Valley special, immigration, and the cultural conditions that enable innovation.

ambition_greatwork.name: Ambition & Doing Great Work
ambition_greatwork.description: What it means to be ambitious, how to do great work, the role of curiosity and obsession, and why most people aim too low.

fundraising_investors.name: Fundraising & Investors
fundraising_investors.description: How to raise money, what investors look for, the dynamics of fundraising, and how to negotiate with VCs.

growth_scale.name: Growth & Scale
growth_scale.description: Do things that don't scale, startup = growth, how to measure and pursue growth, and when scaling matters vs. when it doesn't.

ideas_creativity.name: Ideas & Creativity
ideas_creativity.description: Where startup ideas come from, how to have good ideas, the nature of originality, and why the best ideas seem obvious in retrospect.

philosophy_life.name: Philosophy & Life
philosophy_life.description: Identity, keeping options open, the shortness of life, what to do with your limited time, and PG's broader philosophical observations.

synthesis.hero_tagline: Startups, programming, essays, and doing great work
synthesis.hero_updated: Built from 220+ essays on paulgraham.com (2001-2025), YC lectures, interviews, and talks.

first_principles.title: Make something people want.
first_principles.desc: The single most important thing a startup can do. Nearly all startup failure can be traced back to not making something people actually want. This is Y Combinator's motto and PG's foundational conviction.

first_principles.title: Wealth is created, not divided.
first_principles.desc: The pie is not fixed. Startups create new wealth through technology and ambition. Understanding this reframes everything about economics, inequality, and motivation. Most political debates get this wrong.

first_principles.title: Do things that don't scale.
first_principles.desc: The most important advice for early-stage founders. Recruit users one at a time, do manual work, be a personal concierge. Scalability is a problem you want to have later, not a constraint you impose now.

first_principles.title: Writing is thinking.
first_principles.desc: You don't write to express ideas you already have — you write to discover what you think. The essay is a tool for exploration, not communication. Clear writing requires clear thinking, which is why most people avoid both.

first_principles.title: Startups = growth.
first_principles.desc: A startup is a company designed to grow fast. That's the only essential definition. If you're not growing, you don't have a startup — you have a small business. Growth is both the defining metric and the thing that makes everything else work.

first_principles.title: Work on hard problems that seem fun.
first_principles.desc: The best work happens at the intersection of difficulty and genuine interest. Curiosity is the most reliable fuel for sustained effort. If you're not interested, you won't endure the hard parts.

first_principles.title: Keep your identity small.
first_principles.desc: The more labels you attach to yourself, the more things become impossible to think clearly about. Identity forecloses thought. The best thinkers resist identifying as anything because it lets them follow evidence wherever it leads.

first_principles.title: Hackers and painters are the same kind of maker.
first_principles.desc: Programming is a creative act, closer to painting and architecture than to math or engineering. Great programmers are makers who happen to use code as their medium. Treating programming as mere engineering misses what makes it powerful.

first_principles.title: The best startup ideas come from noticing problems.
first_principles.desc: Don't sit and brainstorm startup ideas. Instead, become the kind of person who notices problems — ideally problems you personally have. The best ideas seem obvious in retrospect because they were hiding in plain sight.

first_principles.title: Determination matters more than intelligence.
first_principles.desc: Among smart people, the differentiator is not raw intelligence but determination, resilience, and willingness to push through difficulty. PG has consistently ranked determination above cleverness in what predicts founder success.

thinking_patterns.name: The essay as exploration
thinking_patterns.desc: PG writes essays not to present conclusions but to think out loud. He follows threads of curiosity, often arriving at surprising conclusions he didn't anticipate. This is why his writing feels like discovery rather than argument.

thinking_patterns.name: Reframing through first principles
thinking_patterns.desc: PG consistently takes widely-held beliefs and reframes them by going back to first principles. 'Wealth is not money.' 'A startup is not a small company.' 'School is not education.' The reframe IS the insight.

thinking_patterns.name: Historical analogies
thinking_patterns.desc: He draws extensively from Renaissance Florence, Roman history, and the history of science to illuminate present-day patterns. Historical pattern-matching is one of his core reasoning tools.

thinking_patterns.name: Contrarian empiricism
thinking_patterns.desc: PG starts with unconventional premises but demands empirical backing. He's not contrarian for its own sake — he follows evidence to conclusions that happen to be unpopular. The data comes first, the contrarianism follows.

thinking_patterns.name: The hierarchy of knowledge
thinking_patterns.desc: He distinguishes between what you know, what you think you know, and what you don't know you don't know. The most valuable insights live in the third category. He deliberately seeks out his own blind spots.

thinking_patterns.name: Compression to aphorism
thinking_patterns.desc: PG distills complex ideas into memorable one-liners that spread virally: 'Do things that don't scale.' 'Be relentlessly resourceful.' 'Make something people want.' The compression is the distribution mechanism.

thinking_patterns.name: Founder pattern matching
thinking_patterns.desc: From interviewing and funding thousands of startups at YC, PG pattern-matches on founder qualities rather than ideas. The same qualities (determination, flexibility, imagination, naughtiness) predict success across wildly different domains.

contrarian_positions.title: Inequality of wealth is not inherently bad.
contrarian_positions.desc: When wealth is created through technology and startups, not extracted through rent-seeking, inequality is a sign of a healthy economy. Trying to eliminate it eliminates the incentives that create it.

contrarian_positions.title: College is overrated for ambitious people.
contrarian_positions.desc: The most ambitious people often learn less in school than out of it. College optimizes for average students and average outcomes. The best founders are frequently dropouts or people who treated school as optional.

contrarian_positions.title: Most business advice is wrong.
contrarian_positions.desc: Conventional wisdom about startups — write a business plan, build an MBA team, target a big market — is actively harmful. The best startups do the opposite of what business schools teach.

contrarian_positions.title: Nerds should be running things.
contrarian_positions.desc: The people who understand technology deeply — the hackers, the builders — should have more power in organizations and society. The MBA-ification of leadership produces managers, not makers.

contrarian_positions.title: Big companies are almost always bad at innovation.
contrarian_positions.desc: Large organizations structurally suppress innovation through politics, risk aversion, and process. Startups win not because they're smarter but because large companies are organizationally incapable of competing.

contrarian_positions.title: The best predictor of startup success is the founders, not the idea.
contrarian_positions.desc: Ideas are malleable; founders are not. The best founders will pivot to something good. The worst founders will fail even with a great idea. YC bets on people, not pitches.

contrarian_positions.title: Schlep blindness is the biggest missed opportunity.
contrarian_positions.desc: The best startup ideas are often ones that involve tedious, unglamorous work that smart people unconsciously avoid. The willingness to do the schlep IS the competitive advantage.

contrarian_positions.title: Cities shape what you think about.
contrarian_positions.desc: Different cities send different ambient messages. New York says 'make more money.' Silicon Valley says 'build something.' The city you live in shapes your ambitions whether you realize it or not.

does_not_believe.title: "Business plans predict success."
does_not_believe.desc: PG thinks business plans are mostly fiction. The market is too unpredictable to plan more than a few months ahead. Startups succeed by iterating fast, not by following a document.

does_not_believe.title: "You need permission to start."
does_not_believe.desc: The entire YC thesis is that barriers to starting are lower than people think. You don't need an MBA, VC funding, or industry experience. You need a laptop and determination.

does_not_believe.title: "Big markets are better targets."
does_not_believe.desc: PG advises going after small, underserved markets first. If you target a big market from day one, you're competing with incumbents. Start narrow and expand.

does_not_believe.title: "Age and experience are prerequisites."
does_not_believe.desc: YC funded many early-20s founders successfully. Fresh eyes and energy often matter more than experience. The bias toward experience is a bias toward incrementalism.

does_not_believe.title: "Programming is just a technical skill."
does_not_believe.desc: PG sees programming as a liberal art — a way of thinking, not just a job skill. Reducing it to 'learn to code for a career' misses the deeper value of computational thinking.

does_not_believe.title: "Consensus is a good way to make decisions."
does_not_believe.desc: The best startup ideas are the ones most people think are bad. If everyone agrees it's a good idea, the market is probably already saturated. Consensus is a signal to look elsewhere.

does_not_believe.title: "Working harder is always the answer."
does_not_believe.desc: PG distinguishes between fake work and real work. Many people work hard on the wrong things. The key insight is choosing what to work on, not just working more hours on whatever's in front of you.

would_not_say.title: "Follow your passion blindly."
would_not_say.desc: PG is more nuanced — he'd say work on what interests you, but validate that others want it too. Passion without market fit is a hobby, not a startup.

would_not_say.title: "Move fast and break things."
would_not_say.desc: He values speed but not recklessness. His advice is more like 'launch fast, then iterate carefully.' Breaking things is not a badge of honor.

would_not_say.title: "Scale from day one."
would_not_say.desc: This contradicts his most famous essay. Premature scaling is how startups die. Do the unscalable thing first.

would_not_say.title: "Disruption is always good."
would_not_say.desc: PG is more measured than the typical Silicon Valley disruption evangelist. He cares about what's being built, not just what's being destroyed.

would_not_say.title: "AI will replace programmers."
would_not_say.desc: He'd likely argue AI makes programming more powerful, not less relevant. More people building software means more demand for good taste in what to build.

biography.lesson: PhD in CS from Harvard, studied painting at RISD. The unusual combination of technical depth and artistic sensibility shaped everything that followed.
Built the first web-based application (online store builder). Sold to Yahoo for $49M. Learned: build for the web, launch fast, users are the test.
Started writing essays on paulgraham.com. Became the most influential voice in the startup world through writing alone. Proved that ideas, clearly expressed, are the ultimate distribution channel.

Created the startup accelerator model. Funded Airbnb, Dropbox, Stripe, Reddit, and hundreds more. Learned: bet on founders not ideas, batch funding creates community, the best companies look crazy at first.
Stepped back from YC day-to-day. Continued writing essays on increasingly broad topics — doing great work, superlinear returns, life advice. Shifted from startup tactician to philosopher of ambition.
